title: Hot / Cold Aisle Guide
summary: Understand airflow separation, intake temperature, exhaust management, and rack cooling effectiveness.

sections.title: What this tool does
body: This tool helps evaluate whether rack airflow is organized to separate cool intake air from hot exhaust air.
bullets: Use it as a planning and sanity-check aid.
Use it to document assumptions before implementation.
Use it to catch thermal issues before equipment overheats.

sections.title: When to use it
body: Use it when planning rack rows, equipment rooms, server rooms, high-density closets, or any space with front-to-back airflow equipment.
bullets: Early thermal planning.
Equipment-room or rack review.
Expansion or heat-load evaluation.
Explaining cooling and airflow tradeoffs to a client, stakeholder, or internal team.

sections.title: Input descriptions
body: These descriptions explain what each input represents in practical thermal and cooling planning terms.

items.label: Rack Orientation
items.description: How racks face relative to each other and airflow.
items.whyItMatters: Orientation controls hot and cold aisle behavior.
items.commonMistake: Pointing equipment exhaust toward another rack intake.

items.label: Intake Side
items.description: Where equipment pulls cool air from.
items.whyItMatters: Intake side should receive the coolest available air.
items.commonMistake: Blocking intake with doors, clutter, or poor layout.

items.label: Exhaust Side
items.whyItMatters: Exhaust should be kept away from intake.
items.commonMistake: Letting hot exhaust recirculate into the front of the rack.

items.label: Aisle Separation
items.description: How well hot and cold air paths are separated.
items.commonMistake: Leaving open rack gaps that allow recirculation.

items.label: Airflow Direction
items.description: The direction equipment fans move air.
items.whyItMatters: Mixed airflow equipment can break aisle assumptions.
items.commonMistake: Assuming every device uses front-to-back airflow.

items.label: Cooling Supply
items.description: Where conditioned air enters the room or aisle.
items.whyItMatters: Supply location affects whether cold air reaches equipment intakes.
items.commonMistake: Cooling the room generally without directing air to the load.

sections.title: How to read the results
body: Healthy means airflow separation appears practical. Watch means recirculation or rack orientation should be reviewed. Risk means hot exhaust may mix with intake air and reduce cooling effectiveness.
bullets: Healthy: the design appears manageable based on the entered assumptions.
Watch: the design may still work, but heat load, airflow, or cooling assumptions deserve review.
Risk: add airflow, reduce heat load, improve cooling, or validate more carefully before implementation.

sections.title: Common mistakes
body: Most thermal problems come from underestimating heat load, assuming air moves freely, or treating room comfort cooling as equipment cooling.
bullets: Ignoring UPS, PSU, PoE, or conversion heat.
Using fan free-air ratings without considering restrictions.
Cooling the room generally instead of managing intake and exhaust paths.
Failing to leave margin for dust, aging fans, warm ambient conditions, and future load.

sections.title: Planning assumptions
body: This guide explains the planning logic behind the tool. It does not replace manufacturer documentation, HVAC design, code requirements, or project-specific engineering judgment.
bullets: Equipment heat should be based on real load when possible.
Cooling capacity should be validated under actual site conditions.
Airflow requires both supply and exhaust paths.
A design with thermal margin is easier to support and expand.

sections.title: Professional responsibility
body: ScopedLabs tools are planning aids. They help surface assumptions and risk, but they do not replace formal engineering review, HVAC review, manufacturer validation, code compliance review, or project-specific design responsibility.
bullets: Validate against actual equipment ratings and site conditions.
Confirm temperature limits and owner expectations.
Document assumptions before implementation.
